coaching.coaching: Pin down fork-winner ordering in plan_review_phase fold with a concrete worked example.
coaching.subject: fork-winner ordering in plan_review_phase fold

coaching.coaching: Run a quick pre-mortem on missing-target drift resolution path under enforcement: how could this plan fail?
coaching.subject: missing-target drift resolution path under enforcement

coaching.coaching: Record an ADR for definition of blocking versus upheld prerequisite findings — it reads like a one-way door.
coaching.subject: definition of blocking versus upheld prerequisite findings

coaching.coaching: Pin down lock-acquisition timeout as distinct failure mode with a concrete worked example.
coaching.subject: lock-acquisition timeout as distinct failure mode

coaching.coaching: Plan how post-rollback compaction snapshot compatibility will be verified in-session — restate any deferred or unobservable success target as an observable proxy.
coaching.subject: post-rollback compaction snapshot compatibility

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the exact code location where the 0.80 execution priority-floor is applied (e.g. inside pass3_decide as an override to the blocking criterion's normal threshold) and how it interacts with novelty_priority_floor and completion_priority_floor.
evidence: Plan: 'During execution, each blocking criterion uses max(its normal threshold, 0.80). Advisory-only criteria stay advisory, deterministic structural failures stay blocking, and lower-priority findings remain visible.'
src/rebar/llm/plan_review/__init__.py lines 211, 369: floor=verify_cfg.novelty_priority_floor and floor=verify_cfg.completion_priority_floor — both applied in pass3.
Plan: 'The 0.80 value is a fixed protocol risk policy for already-started work, not an empirically claimed optimum or project config.' — explicitly NOT a config value, so it is NOT in LLMConfig.
No execution_priority_floor or plan_review_phase check appears anywhere in the codebase.
findings.finding: The plan specifies 'each blocking criterion uses max(its normal threshold, 0.80)' during execution reviews, but does not specify WHERE in the existing multi-pass pipeline this max() is applied — whether in the Pass 1 finder prompt threshold, the Pass 3 decide function, the novelty_priority_floor comparison, or a new execution-specific gate. The existing codebase has both novelty_priority_floor (default 0.4) and completion_priority_floor (default 0.4) as separate config-level scalars applied in pass3 decide; the 0.80 execution floor is a manifest-level field, creating an ambiguous interaction.
findings.location: Settled design / Execution reviews and close gate
scenarios: An implementer adding the 0.80 floor must decide: does max(threshold, 0.80) apply to the Pass 3 novelty drop, the Pass 3 decide blocking threshold, or a new pre-sign check? The plan only says 'blocking criterion threshold' without naming the code location.
If applied incorrectly (e.g. raising only the advisory cap rather than the blocking criterion threshold), execution reviews would be systematically under-flagged for already-started work.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that the plan_review_phase fold must apply only on the fork-WINNING branch (mirroring the existing fork-winner-only pattern for `_fold_claimed_session` and `_fold_close_class` in process_status()), and add this as an explicit implementation constraint in the Settled design section.
evidence: Existing process_status() in _processors.py applies _fold_claimed_session and _fold_close_class ONLY in the fork-WINNER branch (lines ~264-265) and the normal-update branch, NEVER in the existing-chain-wins branch.
The plan does not state that plan_review_phase folding must follow the same fork-winner-only pattern.
A fork where one concurrent STATUS targets 'open' and the other wins with 'in_progress' would incorrectly reset to planning if the losing 'open' event's fold is applied.
findings.finding: The plan_review_phase state-machine fold is not specified to be fork-winner-only, risking incorrect projection when concurrent STATUS events fork. The existing process_status() applies session/close-class folds ONLY on the winning branch (lines 264-265, 290-291 in _processors.py); a naive implementation of plan_review_phase folding that applies on every STATUS event regardless of fork outcome would produce wrong projections (e.g., the loser's 'open' target could incorrectly reset planning when the winning event targeted in_progress).
findings.location: Settled design > Execution reviews and close gate: 'Every committed STATUS event targeting in_progress sets execution. Every committed STATUS event targeting open sets planning.'
scenarios: Two agents concurrently attempt status transitions: one to 'in_progress' (winner) and one to 'open' (loser). If phase folding is applied naively to every event, the loser's 'open' target resets planning even though the winning event set execution.
A force-transition to 'blocked' (which 'preserves the value') while a concurrent 'open' event loses the fork — the loser's fold should not reset planning.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the 'Execution reviews and close gate' section: 'The plan_review_phase fold must be applied exclusively on the fork-winning STATUS branch (matching the existing _fold_claimed_session/fork-winner-only invariant in process_status); the fork-losing branch never updates the projection.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the verdict string(s) compute_validity() returns for pin drift cases (e.g. 'stale-pin-drift' for fingerprint mismatch, 'stale-pin-missing' for deleted target) and add them to the verdict vocabulary alongside the existing named codes.
evidence: Plan: 'At later validity checks, deletion of a previously pinned target is missing-target drift.'
Plan: 'A pinned target that has been deleted or is missing makes the pin stale and requires relationship repair followed by re-review.'

src/rebar/llm/plan_review/attest.py compute_validity(): existing verdict strings are certified, stale-reopened, stale-regver, stale-code, stale-head, stale-material, unverifiable-material — no 'stale-pin', 'missing-target', or similar.
The plan names pin drift as 'drift' generically but never assigns it a verdict code that consumer UI / health derived output can key on.
findings.finding: The plan specifies that 'missing-target drift' (deletion of a previously pinned target) makes a pin stale, and when enforcement is enabled this invalidates the downstream attestation — but the plan does not define the verdict string (reason code) that compute_validity() should return for this case, unlike the exhaustively named existing verdict vocabulary (certified, stale-reopened, stale-regver, stale-code, stale-head, stale-material, unverifiable-material).
findings.location: Settled design / Signed plan-material pins (missing/deleted targets) and Enforcement and rollout
scenarios: The health UI is specified to label drift as 'advisory' when enforcement is off — it must key on a verdict string to do so, but that string is undefined.
The close gate is specified to observe pin drift — it must check a specific verdict code, but no code is defined.
findings.suggested_fix: Add `stale-pin-drift` (pinned fingerprint no longer matches current) and `stale-pin-missing` (pinned target deleted/unresolvable) to the verdict vocabulary and reference them explicitly in the enforcement section and the health-display spec.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace 'blocking' with 'upheld' (or 'verified') in AC18 to match the plan body's Pass 3 terminology: 'Every upheld prerequisite finding carries one verified canonical prerequisite ID.'
evidence: AC18: 'Every blocking prerequisite finding carries one verified canonical ID.'
Plan body: 'Pass 3 preserves prerequisite_id on upheld findings, drops attribution=no candidates as unsupported.'
The plan elsewhere uses 'blocking' for the criterion-level classification (blocking vs. advisory criterion), not for findings that survive verification.
findings.finding: AC18 uses the term 'blocking' for prerequisite findings, but the plan body consistently uses 'upheld' for findings that survive Pass 3; the term 'blocking' is not defined for prerequisite findings and could be confused with the general blocking/advisory criterion classification.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — 'Every blocking prerequisite finding carries one verified canonical ID.'
scenarios: An implementer reads 'blocking' as applying only to findings from blocking-classified criteria, missing that ALL upheld prerequisite findings must carry a verified canonical ID.
A tester skips attribution checks on findings from advisory-classified criteria.
findings.suggested_fix: Change AC18 to: 'Every upheld prerequisite finding carries one verified canonical prerequisite ID (prerequisite_attribution_valid = yes).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that lock-acquisition timeout (LockTimeout) during the pre-sign transaction commit step is classified as retryable unsigned (consistent with the revision-exhaustion case), and document this explicitly in the transaction failure-mode table.
evidence: lock.py raises LockTimeout (returncode=1) when fcntl or mkdir leg cannot be acquired within budget.
The plan's retry/exhaustion path returns 'retryable unsigned' but only describes retries on revision-change; lock-acquisition timeout is not mentioned.
Without explicit classification, a lock timeout during pre-sign could propagate as a CommandError/SigningError rather than retryable unsigned.
findings.finding: The pre-sign transaction does not classify lock-acquisition timeout (LockTimeout from lock.py) as a distinct failure mode. The plan specifies 'retry outside-lock collection at most three times; exhaustion returns retryable unsigned' but does not address what happens when the write-lock acquisition itself times out after a successful revision comparison. In the existing lock infrastructure, LockTimeout raises with returncode=1 and a distinct error message; without a specified mapping, implementers may treat it as a hard error (surfacing to the user as a failure) rather than as 'retryable unsigned' (which would match the exhaustion case's behavior).
findings.location: Settled design > Signed plan-material pins: 'Acquire the global lock and require HEAD still equals revision-after … On revision change retry outside-lock collection at most three times; exhaustion returns retryable unsigned.'
scenarios: Under heavy write contention, the global lock cannot be acquired within the 30s×2 budget during the pre-sign transaction commit step. The plan does not say whether this yields retryable unsigned or a hard user-visible error.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the pre-sign transaction description: 'A lock-acquisition timeout during the commit step is classified as retryable unsigned (matching the revision-exhaustion outcome), so no operation is wedged.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Assert the post-rollback schema state for compaction snapshots containing plan_review_phase: explicitly state whether older readers skip unknown projection fields, and confirm no data loss or deserialization failure occurs on rollback (DATA LOSS RISK if unaddressed).
evidence: 'Operational rollback is disabling both enforcement flags before or with a code rollback; no stored attestation must be rewritten.'
No statement about what pre-rollback code does when it encounters compaction snapshots containing the plan_review_phase projection field.
Contrast with manifest pin lines, which ARE explicitly addressed: 'an older verifier authenticates that stored list including unknown pin lines'.
findings.finding: The post-rollback schema state for compaction snapshots that already contain the plan_review_phase field is not specified. The rollback procedure ('disable enforcement flags, then rollback code') leaves older code reading snapshots with an unknown projection field; the plan does not assert whether older readers skip, error on, or misinterpret this field, nor does it confirm data integrity is preserved.
findings.location: Settled design > Enforcement and rollout — 'Operational rollback is disabling both enforcement flags before or with a code rollback; no stored attestation must be rewritten.'
scenarios: Code is rolled back after some tickets have been compacted with the plan_review_phase field in their snapshots; older reducer code may fail to deserialize these snapshots, fall back to re-reading full history (performance regression), or silently produce incorrect compiled state.
A partial rollback (some nodes on old code, some on new) creates inconsistent compiled-state reads from the same snapshot store.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a note parallel to the manifest-pin backward-compatibility statement: e.g., 'The snapshot format is schema-versioned; unknown projection fields are ignored by older reducers, which recompute missing projections from event history.' If older reducers do not ignore unknown fields, require a two-phase rollout where the snapshot format is not changed until older readers are proven forward-compatible.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the derived-health contract schema (field names, types, and semantics) in the Settled Design so that all surfaces implement the same object and AC #20 is objectively verifiable.
evidence: Plan names surfaces (CLI show, MCP ticket detail, audit output, close errors, web ticket-review UI) and behaviors ('labels drift advisory', 'derived at read time') but never defines the contract's fields or schema.
AC #20 says 'consume the shared derived-health contract' — 'shared' implies a single interface definition, yet no such definition appears anywhere in the plan.
Without a defined schema, an executor must invent the contract, and a reviewer cannot determine whether a given implementation 'consumes' it correctly.
findings.finding: The 'shared derived-health contract' referenced in AC #20 and the Health & Documentation section is never defined with a concrete schema — field names, data types, and the exact shape of the derived object are absent. An executor implementing the multi-surface health display and a reviewer evaluating AC #20 both need this definition to proceed and to verify compliance respectively.
findings.location: Health and documentation section; AC: 'All detailed review surfaces consume the shared derived-health contract.'
scenarios: An executor implementing the MCP ticket-detail surface asks: what fields does the derived-health object expose — is it {pin_drift: bool, advisory: bool, phase: str} or something else?
A reviewer checking AC #20 cannot determine compliance without knowing what 'the contract' requires.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Derived health record' subsection under Health and documentation that enumerates the contract, e.g.: `{ pin_drift: bool, pin_drift_advisory: bool, phase: planning|execution, floor: float|null, enforcement_active: bool }` — each surface then maps these fields to its own presentation layer, and AC #20 is satisfied when all named surfaces consume this record type.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC that verifies a malformed pin, review-phase, or priority-floor line causes the attestation to be evaluated as malformed/invalid (not silently ignored or treated as legacy).
evidence: "New parsers raise one ManifestFormatError on duplicate, malformed, unknown-enum, missing-token, extra-token, or invalid numeric lines. compute_plan_review_validity catches this error once and returns malformed/invalid, so consumers never choose separate fallbacks."
No AC text covers malformed-line detection or the malformed/invalid return path.
findings.finding: The described behavior that malformed pin/review-phase/floor lines cause `compute_plan_review_validity` to return `malformed/invalid` (via `ManifestFormatError`) has no covering acceptance criterion.
findings.location: Settled design / Signed plan-material pins — 'New parsers raise one ManifestFormatError on duplicate, malformed, unknown-enum, missing-token, extra-token, or invalid numeric lines. compute_plan_review_validity catches this error once and returns malformed/invalid'
scenarios: An implementer adds the parsers but wires the error to a fallback (e.g. treats parse failure as legacy/unpinned) rather than malformed/invalid — no AC catches the regression.
A future refactor splits the error catch point; no AC verifies the single-catch contract.
findings.suggested_fix: Add: 'A plan-material-pin, review-phase, or priority-floor line that is malformed, duplicated, or carries an unknown enum value causes attestation evaluation to return malformed/invalid.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC that verifies unresolvable or unreadable relationship targets (missing, ambiguous, canonical-ID mismatch, reducer error) abort the review attempt before any LLM call is made.
evidence: "A target is resolvable and readable only when its stored reference canonicalizes to exactly one existing reducer-readable ticket state... Missing/deleted targets, ambiguous or malformed references, reducer errors, or canonical-ID mismatch are unresolved/unreadable and stop review before any LLM call."
No AC covers the stop-before-LLM contract or the resolution precondition.
findings.finding: The described behavior that unresolvable/unreadable pinned targets (missing, ambiguous, canonical-ID mismatch, reducer errors) abort the review before any LLM call has no covering acceptance criterion.
findings.location: Settled design / Signed plan-material pins — 'Missing/deleted targets, ambiguous or malformed references, reducer errors, or canonical-ID mismatch are unresolved/unreadable and stop review before any LLM call.'
scenarios: An implementation resolves targets lazily (after the LLM call) and silently treats unresolvable targets as absent pins — no AC catches this.
A misresolved canonical ID produces a pin for the wrong ticket; no AC verifies the mismatch check.
findings.suggested_fix: Add: 'A review attempt whose direct-child or prerequisite relationship target is unresolvable or unreadable (missing, ambiguous reference, canonical-ID mismatch, or reducer error) is rejected before any LLM call, returning an unsigned error result.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC that verifies a review containing any indeterminate prerequisite coverage record produces an unsigned INDETERMINATE result rather than a signed PASS.
evidence: "indeterminate is used only when specialized model evaluation, output parsing, or attribution cannot complete. It never asserts a blocking incompatibility, but complete prerequisite coverage is required for certification, so any indeterminate record yields an unsigned INDETERMINATE review rather than signed PASS."
AC17 covers 'every readable prerequisite receives one three-disposition coverage record' but does not verify that an indeterminate record blocks signing.
findings.finding: The described behavior that any indeterminate prerequisite coverage record causes the review to produce an unsigned INDETERMINATE rather than a signed PASS has no covering acceptance criterion.
findings.location: Settled design / Signed plan-material pins — 'complete prerequisite coverage is required for certification, so any indeterminate record yields an unsigned INDETERMINATE review rather than signed PASS'
scenarios: An implementation treats indeterminate like consistent (no findings) and signs a PASS — AC17 is satisfied (a record exists) but the signing gate is bypassed.
After a model escalation fails to produce parseable output, the review is signed anyway; no AC catches this.
findings.suggested_fix: Add: 'A plan review that includes one or more indeterminate prerequisite coverage records is not signed and is returned as unsigned INDETERMINATE, even when all other criteria pass.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Expand AC7 to inline the consumer list: 'Enabled pin enforcement invalidates claim, close, attestation reuse, drift refresh, re-sign, audit, and derived health after related material drift.'
evidence: AC text: 'Enabled pin enforcement invalidates every named consumer after related material drift.'
Plan body: 'Pin validity uses one implementation wherever an attestation is already being evaluated: claim, close, attestation reuse, drift refresh, re-sign, audit, and derived health.'
'Named' implicitly cross-references this list but the AC is self-contained prose.
findings.finding: AC7 references 'every named consumer' without naming them, while the plan body enumerates the specific consumers explicitly; this creates a verifiability gap where an implementer cannot confirm completeness from the AC alone.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — 'Enabled pin enforcement invalidates every named consumer after related material drift.'
scenarios: A tester reads only the AC list and cannot determine which consumers to exercise without hunting the body text.
A new consumer added later is not automatically covered by the AC's 'named' qualifier.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'every named consumer' with 'claim, close, attestation reuse, drift refresh, re-sign, audit, and derived health'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a calibration step (e.g., sample N historical plan-review scores on in-progress tickets) to empirically validate the 0.80 floor before it is frozen as protocol, or document the explicit policy rationale and acceptance that a future adjustment will invalidate existing execution attestations.
evidence: 'The 0.80 value is a fixed protocol risk policy for already-started work, not an empirically claimed optimum or project config.'
No spike, benchmark, or calibration experiment is mentioned for this threshold.
AC: 'Execution reviews apply the fixed 0.80 priority-floor.'
findings.finding: The 0.80 execution priority-floor is asserted as a fixed protocol risk policy with no measurement plan or empirical basis. The plan explicitly states it is 'not an empirically claimed optimum,' yet no spike, historical-review sampling, or calibration experiment is planned to validate whether 0.80 separates acceptable from unacceptable risk for already-started work.
findings.location: Settled design — Execution reviews and close gate: 'The 0.80 value is a fixed protocol risk policy for already-started work, not an empirically claimed optimum or project config.'
scenarios: If 0.80 is too high, legitimate execution reviews fail the floor and force unnecessary re-reviews, creating friction on in-progress work.
If 0.80 is too low, the floor provides no real quality signal over planning reviews, defeating its purpose.
When the threshold is later raised via policy, all prior execution attestations become invalid — the cost depends on whether the original value was well-calibrated.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either expose the execution priority-floor as a per-project config (like enforce_plan_material_pins) so projects can tune it without a protocol change, or explicitly document the deliberate decision to keep it protocol-only and accept that calibration changes will invalidate existing execution attestations.
evidence: 'a future required floor compares numerically, so raising policy makes older execution attestations require re-review while lowering it accepts attestations carrying the older higher floor'
'not an empirically claimed optimum or project config' — explicitly non-configurable
The plan describes evolution as a code/protocol change rather than a config change.
findings.finding: The 0.80 priority-floor is hardcoded as a non-configurable protocol constant even though the plan explicitly acknowledges it is expected to evolve ('a future required floor compares numerically, so raising policy makes older execution attestations require re-review'). A threshold the plan itself anticipates will change is a T5e changeability gap when it is not configurable.
findings.location: Settled design — Execution reviews and close gate: 'The 0.80 value is a fixed protocol risk policy for already-started work, not an empirically claimed optimum or project config. The numeric manifest field is evolution-safe: a future required floor compares numerically...'
scenarios: If the rebar project wants to pilot a stricter 0.90 floor while the base default stays at 0.80, there is no mechanism to do so without a code change.
If operational experience shows 0.80 is wrong, the fix is a protocol bump that invalidates all existing execution attestations — higher blast radius than a config change would have.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a verify.execution_priority_floor config key defaulting to 0.80, validated to [0.0, 1.0]; manifests record the floor actually applied so older attestations remain valid against the floor they were signed with.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an ADR (or AGENTS.md / design-doc section) capturing the key architectural decisions introduced by this epic: natural vs. eager propagation rationale, the plan_review_phase projection lifecycle rules, the three-disposition prerequisite schema, and the byte-for-byte DSSE manifest storage contract.
evidence: No mention of ADR, AGENTS.md, or design-doc update anywhere in the plan text.
'plan-material-pin is documented in advanced attestation/gate documentation, not basic user documentation' — this covers user docs only, not architectural decision records.

Novel decisions include: pin manifest syntax, propagation model choice (natural vs eager), phase projection lifecycle, three-disposition prerequisite schema, DSSE manifest byte-for-byte storage contract.
findings.finding: The plan introduces multiple novel architectural decisions — signed plan-material pins, the natural (non-eager) propagation model, the plan_review_phase reducer projection, the prerequisite coverage record schema (three-disposition), and the phase-aware manifest format — but no ADR, AGENTS.md update, or design-doc update is mentioned anywhere in the plan.
findings.location: Settled design (all sub-sections); Health and documentation section; Not in scope section
scenarios: A future maintainer adding a new lifecycle consumer must reverse-engineer the pin propagation model from code rather than consulting a decision record.
When a later team considers eager propagation, there is no documented rationale for why natural propagation was chosen, risking re-litigating the decision.
Onboarding a new contributor to the attestation subsystem requires reading multiple plan documents rather than a single authoritative ADR.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify that a contiguous-halves recursive bisection splitter either already exists in sizing.py or add a spec for it, distinct from the existing greedy pack_container_bins, covering the stable-order invariant and the terminal condition (singleton that still exceeds the largest window).
evidence: 'A provider context-limit error splits the failing stable-order bin into contiguous halves and retries recursively.'

sizing.pack_container_bins() in src/rebar/llm/plan_review/sizing.py is a greedy first-fit algorithm (lines 48–76), not a bisection splitter — no contiguous-halves recursive split exists in the codebase.
The existing child fan-out in pass1.py uses pack_container_bins + a too-big sentinel, with no recursive halving step.
findings.finding: The prerequisite evaluation 'contiguous halves recursive retry' on context-limit errors is an unverified capability assertion. The plan states: 'A provider context-limit error splits the failing stable-order bin into contiguous halves and retries recursively.' The existing bin-packing infrastructure (sizing.pack_container_bins) is a greedy first-fit algorithm that produces bins, not a bisection splitter. No bisection/recursive-halving primitive exists in the sizing or orchestrator modules. This is a novel algorithm the plan asserts exists but does not.
findings.location: Settled design > Prerequisite consistency review (paragraph: 'Reuse sizing.container_budget()...')
scenarios: An implementer assumes a halving primitive exists, discovers it doesn't, and must design it from scratch — creating a de-facto novel algorithm with no fidelity gate for the halving path itself.

The halving interacts with the 'stable-order' requirement: halving a bin can produce an odd-sized remainder; the plan does not specify the invariant that must hold across splits (e.g. whether attribution can straddle a split boundary).
findings.suggested_fix: Reference the exact new function name (e.g. `sizing.split_bin_halves`) and add it to the prerequisite evaluation size-handling design section, explicitly distinguishing it from `pack_container_bins`. Alternatively, reuse the existing greedy packing approach and drop the contiguous-halves language if bisection is not actually required.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a brief prior-art note (one paragraph or ADR reference) acknowledging in-toto link attestations and/or SLSA dependency provenance as the closest established approaches, and recording why the manifest-line extension is preferred (e.g. no new dependency, backward-compatible, computed-on-read rather than write-time graph mutation).
evidence: 'Signed pins extend the existing computed-on-read attestation-validity model: the downstream review cryptographically records exactly what related plan material it relied on' — this is structurally identical to in-toto link attestations binding artifact digests.

ADR 0044 cites 'in-toto attestation' and 'SLSA' as prior art for the signing substrate itself, but neither this plan nor the 'Why this approach' section addresses whether in-toto links or SLSA provenance predicates could serve the cross-ticket dependency-pinning need directly.
ABSENCE: No ADR or plan section discusses why a custom manifest-line schema is preferred over an in-toto predicate extension or SLSA dependency field.
findings.finding: The cross-ticket dependency-pinning pattern ('signed plan-material pins') is a novel architectural approach with no discussion of well-established prior art: in-toto's `link` metadata (which cryptographically binds supply-chain steps by artifact hashes across producers/consumers), SLSA provenance attestations (which chain build steps by digest), and build-system incremental invalidation (Bazel, Nix) all solve structurally similar 'does downstream still match upstream inputs' problems. The plan does not consider these before committing to its bespoke manifest-line approach.
findings.location: Settled design > Signed plan-material pins; Why this approach
scenarios: A future audit asks why the project invented bespoke cross-ticket pinning when in-toto link metadata or SLSA dependency fields exist — the plan has no recorded answer.
An implementer later discovers that the custom pin format duplicates semantics already in the in-toto/SLSA ecosystem, creating future interoperability friction.
findings.suggested_fix: In the 'Why this approach' section, add: 'In-toto link metadata and SLSA provenance predicates solve an analogous cross-step input-binding problem in supply chains; they are not adopted here because [no new dependency / manifest-line extension is sufficient for single-hop pinning / rebar's DSSE substrate already provides the signing layer without requiring a full in-toto statement per pin].' This satisfies the prior-art consideration criterion without changing the design.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify how the reducer-cache version bump re-reduction is batched or rate-limited (e.g., lazy per-ticket on first access with a background sweep, or an explicit staged migration job) to avoid an unbounded fan-out on upgrade.
evidence: 'A reducer-cache version bump makes the rule apply on first post-upgrade reduction; the derived value is then carried forward idempotently in later snapshots.'
No mention of batching, rate-limiting, background job, or staged rollout for the initial re-reduction sweep.
findings.finding: The reducer-cache version bump that bootstraps plan_review_phase triggers re-reduction of every ticket on first post-upgrade access, but the plan specifies no batching or throttling for this bulk re-reduction. On a large tracker with many tickets this becomes a single unbounded fan-out operation.
findings.location: Settled design > Execution reviews and close gate — 'A reducer-cache version bump makes the rule apply on first post-upgrade reduction'
scenarios: A tracker with thousands of tickets causes a thundering-herd of reductions on the first request after deployment, potentially blocking normal operations or hitting memory/CPU limits.
Deployment rollout stalls mid-way, leaving some tickets re-reduced and others not — while idempotent, the unbounded per-request cost is uncontrolled.
findings.suggested_fix: Clarify that re-reduction is purely lazy (triggered only on the first read of each individual ticket, not a proactive sweep), or add a background migration job that processes tickets in small batches with a configurable concurrency limit, and document expected wall-clock duration at representative scale.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a JSON schema for the prerequisite coverage record (plan_review_prerequisite_coverage.schema.json or equivalent) that formalizes {prerequisite_id, disposition: enum[consistent|finding|indeterminate], findings[]} and extend the Pass 2 verification schema to include optional prerequisite_attribution_valid: enum[yes|no|na].
evidence: Plan: 'Pass 1 returns exactly one coverage record per canonical prerequisite: {prerequisite_id, disposition, findings}, where disposition is consistent, finding, or indeterminate.'
Plan: 'The shared index-keyed Pass 2 verification schema gains optional prerequisite_attribution_valid = yes|no|na; non-prerequisite findings use na.'
grep of src/rebar/schemas/ for 'prerequisite_id', 'prerequisite_attribution_valid', 'attribution_valid', 'consistent', 'indeterminate' (as enum values): zero matches.
No schema file named plan_review_prerequisite_coverage or similar exists in src/rebar/schemas/.
findings.finding: The prerequisite coverage record schema ({prerequisite_id, disposition, findings}) is referenced throughout the plan as the output of the specialized criterion's Pass 1, but no JSON schema file is specified or named for it, and the `disposition` enum vocabulary (consistent | finding | indeterminate) and the `prerequisite_attribution_valid` field (yes | no | na) added to the Pass 2 verification schema are defined only in prose — not in any existing or named schema file in src/rebar/schemas/.
findings.location: Settled design / Prerequisite consistency review
scenarios: An implementer building the specialized criterion has no authoritative schema to validate Pass 1 output against, leaving the {prerequisite_id, disposition, findings} shape and the disposition enum values unvalidated at runtime.
The Pass 2 verifier schema extension for prerequisite_attribution_valid has no authoritative definition, so the yes|no|na enum could drift between the verifier prompt and the decide/drop logic.
findings.suggested_fix: Create src/rebar/schemas/plan_review_prerequisite_coverage.schema.json with `required: [prerequisite_id, disposition]`, `disposition` as a strict enum of `["consistent", "finding", "indeterminate"]`, and `findings` as an array of `$ref: common.schema.json#/$defs/finding`. Add `prerequisite_attribution_valid` as an optional field with `enum: ["yes", "no", "na"]` to the Pass 2 verification record schema.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define ManifestFormatError (its class and module location) and co-locate per-parser ManifestFormatError trigger rules with each parser's specification (e.g. which duplicate condition for pins: same role+id pair? any two review-phase lines?).
evidence: Plan: 'Add manifest_review_phase(), manifest_priority_floor(), and manifest_pins() parsers beside existing manifest parsers; every lifecycle consumer uses them.'

Plan: 'New parsers raise one ManifestFormatError on duplicate, malformed, unknown-enum, missing-token, extra-token, or invalid numeric lines. manifest_pins() returns [] only when no pin lines exist; manifest_priority_floor() returns None only when no floor line exists; manifest_review_phase() returns planning only when the phase line is absent.'
src/rebar/llm/plan_review/manifest.py: no manifest_pins, manifest_review_phase, or manifest_priority_floor functions exist.
The ManifestFormatError type is not defined in manifest.py or attest.py (search returned no matches).
findings.finding: The three new manifest parsers the plan requires — manifest_pins(), manifest_review_phase(), and manifest_priority_floor() — are named and their return conventions are described (manifest_pins() returns [], manifest_review_phase() returns 'planning', manifest_priority_floor() returns None on absence), but the plan does not specify these parsers' precise ManifestFormatError trigger conditions per parser. The existing manifest.py has no such parsers, and the plan co-locates the error-trigger rule ('New parsers raise one ManifestFormatError on duplicate, malformed, unknown-enum, missing-token, extra-token, or invalid numeric lines') only in the pin-syntax sub-section — not alongside each parser's definition or the compute_validity call-site.
findings.location: Settled design / Signed plan-material pins (parser specification)
scenarios: An implementer must infer which exact lines trigger duplicate-detection for manifest_pins() (two pins with same role+id? two review-phase lines?) without a co-located specification.
The ManifestFormatError type itself is not defined in the codebase; its exception class must be created, but the plan does not specify where it lives (manifest.py? a new errors module?).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the new SCHEMA_VERSION value, the make_initial_state() field addition for plan_review_phase (default 'planning'), and the process_snapshot() restoration step so the projection survives compaction and bootstraps correctly.
evidence: Plan: 'Ticket review phase is an internal reducer-maintained plan_review_phase projection… carried in compiled state and compaction snapshots.'
Plan: 'A reducer-cache version bump makes the rule apply on first post-upgrade reduction; the derived value is then carried forward idempotently in later snapshots.'
src/rebar/reducer/_state.py make_initial_state(): no plan_review_phase field is seeded.
src/rebar/reducer/_version.py SCHEMA_VERSION = 5: no mention of plan_review_phase.
src/rebar/reducer/_processors.py process_status(): no plan_review_phase update on in_progress or open transitions.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a new reducer-maintained plan_review_phase projection (values: planning | execution) that must be carried in compiled state and compaction snapshots, and a reducer-cache version bump is specified to trigger bootstrap on first post-upgrade reduction — but the plan never specifies the new SCHEMA_VERSION value, how process_snapshot() restores plan_review_phase, or how make_initial_state() is extended to seed the field.
findings.location: Settled design / Execution reviews and close gate (plan_review_phase projection)
scenarios: Without a specified new SCHEMA_VERSION, the version bump that triggers bootstrapping is undefined — an implementer could pick any value or forget to bump.
Without specifying make_initial_state() extension, a pre-feature SNAPSHOT that lacks plan_review_phase will fail the bootstrap rule silently (key absent vs. key present with 'planning').
process_snapshot() must restore plan_review_phase from compiled_state, but the plan does not specify this handler update, potentially leaving the projection non-surviving after compaction.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit dependency of acaf-bb4d-9eba-470c on 5967-4476-7f9f-442d (or vice-versa) and add a combined manifest-order integration test asserting the full parent-defined field sequence is produced by build_manifest with both modifications present.
evidence: 5967: 'In build_manifest final order, insert review-phase immediately after advisory, then optional priority-floor, before rebar-version.'
acaf: 'Extend the deterministic manifest so pin lines appear after optional verified-at-sha and before dep lines: plan-material-pin: <role> <canonical-ticket-id> <material-fingerprint>.'
Neither child names the other as a prerequisite or states that the final assembled manifest order requires both changes to be present simultaneously.

If one child lands first and its tests run in isolation, build_manifest will be in a partially-modified state; the other child's insertion point may be computed relative to absent fields, producing a mis-ordered manifest that fails deterministic round-trip tests only once both are merged.
findings.finding: Both 5967-4476-7f9f-442d and acaf-bb4d-9eba-470c independently modify the same build_manifest function at different positions, with neither child stating a dependency on or coordination requirement with the other.
findings.location: child 5967-4476-7f9f-442d (Implementation: 'In build_manifest final order, insert review-phase…') and child acaf-bb4d-9eba-470c (Implementation: 'Extend the deterministic manifest so pin lines appear after optional verified-at-sha…')
scenarios: Concurrent development: agent A finishes 5967 and merges; agent B implements acaf against the updated function but inserts pin lines using an insertion-point assumption that was invalidated by A's earlier edits, causing byte-order or position regression in the combined manifest.

Integration testing in 145e fails because the full deterministic order (parent: '…advisory, review-phase, optional priority-floor, rebar-version, …, optional verified-at-sha, zero or more plan-material-pin lines, then dep lines') requires both modifications to be coherent — a property neither child alone tests.
findings.suggested_fix: State in acaf's Implementation that it depends on 5967 for the review-phase and priority-floor positions being already present in build_manifest before pin lines are appended; add one deterministic manifest-order test covering the complete parent-specified sequence to the acaf or 145e verification suite.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add explicit predecessor dependencies: 7c27 depends on acaf; 2bbd and cb6b depend on 75de — so that shared contract boundaries are stable before consumers are implemented.
evidence: 7c27: 'Consume the pin story's PlanRelationSnapshot contract; use it only to construct isolated specialized-criterion inputs.' — PlanRelationSnapshot is defined and owned by acaf, which is a sibling with no stated ordering.

2bbd: 'Call the validity story's compute_plan_review_validity close profile for task, story, and epic close operations. Do not duplicate phase or pin comparison in transition code.' — compute_plan_review_validity is owned by 75de, also a sibling with no stated ordering.

cb6b: 'Consume the validity story's DerivedPlanReviewHealth contract in detailed CLI show, MCP ticket detail, audit output, close errors, and the web ticket-review UI.' — DerivedPlanReviewHealth is owned by 75de, again a sibling with no stated ordering.
The sibling roster lists all seven children as peers; no ticket expresses a depends_on relationship to establish implementation sequencing.
findings.finding: Child 7c27-10fc-4fe6-4b56 has a hard API dependency on acaf-bb4d-9eba-470c's PlanRelationSnapshot contract, child 2bbd-87f6-8765-4b00 has a hard API dependency on 75de-02b0-d454-410b's compute_plan_review_validity close profile, and child cb6b-24b4-1056-4ee6 has a hard API dependency on 75de-02b0-d454-410b's DerivedPlanReviewHealth contract — but none of these three children states the upstream child as a named prerequisite or establishes an ordering constraint.
findings.location: child 7c27-10fc-4fe6-4b56 (Implementation: 'Consume the pin story's PlanRelationSnapshot contract'); child 2bbd-87f6-8765-4b00 (Implementation: 'Call the validity story's compute_plan_review_validity close profile'); child cb6b-24b4-1056-4ee6 (Implementation: 'Consume the validity story's DerivedPlanReviewHealth contract')
scenarios: An agent picks up 7c27 before acaf has stabilised PlanRelationSnapshot; the criterion is built against an assumed interface that changes when acaf is finalised, requiring rework.
2bbd is implemented before 75de defines compute_plan_review_validity's close-profile signature; 2bbd either duplicates logic (violating its own non-duplication directive) or cannot compile/test.
cb6b's rendering tests are written before DerivedPlanReviewHealth's status enum is fixed by 75de; a later status-string change in 75de breaks cb6b's golden tests without a stated contract boundary.
findings.suggested_fix: In each dependent child's ticket, add a 'Depends on' line (or equivalent relation) naming the upstream sibling that owns the consumed contract, and add one interface-contract unit test in the dependent child that imports the real type from the upstream module rather than defining its own stub.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add explicit depends_on relationships from 145e-52a9-26e3-4209 to each of the six implementation siblings (acaf, 75de, 5967, 2bbd, 7c27, cb6b) so it cannot be claimed or closed until all prerequisites are certified.
evidence: 145e's lifecycle scenarios directly exercise features delivered by acaf (pin emission), 75de (enforcement and drift health), 5967 (phase transitions), 2bbd (close gate), 7c27 (prerequisite criterion), and cb6b (surface rendering).
145e lists no depends_on relationship to any of the six implementation siblings.
'Run the complete lifecycle interface suite with both rebar policies enabled' requires all implementation siblings to have stable, merged behaviour.
The Verification step 'Run a real review-plan and claim/close dry run on fixture tickets and record the observed gate health on this ticket' is impossible if any upstream child is incomplete.
findings.finding: Child 145e-52a9-26e3-4209 (the integration/dogfood child) must demonstrate correct behaviour of all prior implementation children but states no dependency on any of them, creating an implicit ordering gap where 145e could be attempted before its prerequisites are complete.
findings.location: child 145e-52a9-26e3-4209 (Goal: 'Prove the integrated behavior through public surfaces'; Implementation: 'Add public-surface lifecycle scenarios for: legacy unpinned attestations; emission while enforcement is off; …')
scenarios: An agent attempts 145e as soon as the epic is claimed; all six upstream children are still open, so the lifecycle scenarios cannot pass and the agent must block or produce a misleading partial result.
145e is closed based on partial upstream completion, providing false assurance that the full integrated behaviour is verified.
findings.suggested_fix: In 145e's ticket, set depends_on: [acaf-bb4d-9eba-470c, 75de-02b0-d454-410b, 5967-4476-7f9f-442d, 2bbd-87f6-8765-4b00, 7c27-10fc-4fe6-4b56, cb6b-24b4-1056-4ee6] and note in Implementation that the lifecycle suite must be run only after all upstream siblings reach certified status.

findings.finding: Phase-compatibility enforcement at validity-check time (claim, close, reuse) is not covered by any child's acceptance criteria. 75de owns compute_plan_review_validity and its implementation text says the close profile retains a 'phase' check, but 75de's ACs only enumerate pin-drift invalidation scenarios. 5967's phase-compatibility ACs ('Planning mode rejects execution-phase attestations', 'Execution mode accepts planning/execution attestations') are scoped to the review-signing path, not the post-sign validity evaluation that claim, close, and reuse perform. 2bbd delegates to 75de ('Call the validity story's compute_plan_review_validity close profile') and asserts 'Enabled close enforcement accepts only certified, phase-compatible reviews', but no AC in 75de or 2bbd verifies that an attestation signed with an incompatible phase is actually rejected at close/claim/reuse time.
findings.location: child 75de-02b0-d454-410b (compute_plan_review_validity close profile ACs) and child 2bbd-87f6-8765-4b00 (close gate AC)
findings.suggested_fix: In 75de's AC list add: 'compute_plan_review_validity rejects an attestation whose signed review-phase is incompatible with the ticket's current execution mode at every consumer call site (claim, close, reuse, re-sign, audit).' In 2bbd's AC list add: 'Close returns a phase-incompatibility error and review-plan remediation when the existing attestation's phase is incompatible, even when pin enforcement is disabled.'

findings.finding: The plan's pre-sign OCC mechanism asserts 'a ticket-store generation token' that does not exist in the codebase. The plan says: 're-collect all dimensions outside the lock with a ticket-store generation token. Acquire the global write lock, verify the generation is unchanged.' No such token is defined anywhere in the store primitives (`_store/lock.py`, `reducer/_cache.py`, `reducer/_api.py`). The existing store offers: (a) the write lock for mutual exclusion, (b) dir-hash for reducer-cache invalidation, and (c) git commit SHAs on the tickets branch. The plan never resolves which of these (or a new primitive) serves as the 'generation token', nor whether a diff between pre-LLM and post-LLM snapshots of multiple tickets' dir-hashes is the intended mechanism. This is a novel OCC pattern applied to a git-backed event-sourced store that has prior art (git's own atomic commit boundary as a per-ticket-dir mtime/inode snapshot, or the tracker branch HEAD SHA) — none of which are evaluated or selected.
findings.location: Settled design § Signed plan-material pins — 'The phase story owns one unified pre-sign transaction … re-collect all dimensions outside the lock with a ticket-store generation token. Acquire the global write lock, verify the generation is unchanged'
findings.suggested_fix: The simplest grounded option: define the generation token as a dict of `{ticket_id: dir_hash}` for the subject ticket and each pinned related ticket, captured before the LLM call using the existing `reducer/_cache.py` dir-hash computation. Under the write lock, recompute these dir-hashes and compare; abort if any diverged. This reuses an existing primitive and avoids a new git operation.

findings.finding: The plan describes a 'ticket-store generation token' as the change-detection primitive for the pre-sign retry loop, but no such global store-generation counter exists in the codebase. The actual mechanism is per-ticket dir_hashes (filename+size+mtime in `reducer/_cache.py`). The plan's language implies a single token covering subject + all related tickets, but the implementation would require snapshotting N independent per-ticket dir_hashes. An implementer searching for a 'generation token' API will not find one, and the discrepancy could cause them to either add unnecessary infrastructure or silently use an incorrect mechanism.
findings.location: Settled design / Signed plan-material pins: 'The phase story owns one unified pre-sign transaction... Acquire the global write lock, verify the generation is unchanged...'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'a ticket-store generation token' with 'a per-ticket dir_hash snapshot ({ticket_id: dir_hash} for subject and all related tickets, computed via `compute_dir_hash`)'. Specify that the lock-held verification step re-computes each dir_hash and compares them to the pre-LLM snapshot.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
